title: Chapter 29: The Case
content: Midnight hits and the phone breathes.

I'm watching the Mephisto icon like it's a wound I'm waiting to reopen, and right at 00:00:00 — not a millisecond early, not a millisecond late — the red glow fractures. Shifts. The icon doesn't disappear; it transforms, red bleeding into amber like a sunset running backward, and the interface I've been staring at for three years collapses into something new.
DATA AUDIT MODE
The words resolve across my screen in a font I've never seen — clean, monospaced, almost beautiful. Clinical. Like the system is embarrassed to be seen without its makeup.

"Holy shit," Leo says from behind my shoulder, and I feel his breath warm against my neck, quick and shallow. He's been hovering since eleven forty-five, laptop open, unDRM code idle on his screen, waiting for me to crack the door so he can slip through behind me.
I don't say anything. I'm reading.

The file structure unfurls like a map of a country that shouldn't exist. Nested directories, perfectly organized, labeled by function in lowercase with underscores, the way a meticulous engineer would name them or a medieval cartographer would name the circles of Hell. Same thing, probably.

/harvest/
/processing/
/archive/
/jurisdiction/
/enforcement/

Five root directories. The entire architecture of human extraction, sitting on my phone like a corporate org chart. I zoom out slightly and see the subdirectories branching from each one — hundreds, thousands of paths threading downward into the dark. It's beautiful in the way a carnivorous plant is beautiful. Symmetrical. Efficient. Designed to attract and consume.

The amber light of the screen catches the edge of my thumb and I notice my hand isn't shaking. Of course it isn't. Steady hands. Always steady now. The irony isn't lost on me — or it would be, if I could feel irony.

"The structure," I say, and my voice comes out flat, diagnostic. "It's organized by workflow. Harvest feeds processing, processing feeds archive. Jurisdiction governs the contract enforcement, enforcement executes the deductions. It's a pipeline."
"A soul pipeline," Leo mutters.
"A data pipeline. Souls are the brand name." I tap /archive/ and the directory opens.
Inside: directories for every Creator who ever signed.

They're listed alphabetically, thousands of them, each one a folder named with the convention LAST_INITIAL_ID_YEAR. The scroll goes on forever. Aarons and Abbots and Adamses, Baileys and Bakers and Barnses, an entire phone book of the harvested, the archived, the filed-away. People who gave up joy for rent money, who traded wonder for a shot at something better, who signed on the digital line because the alternative was slower and less dignified.
I scroll to H.
/HART_CH_2024/
My name in the machine. My folder. My file.
I open it.

The contents render as a list — clean, organized, timestamped. Each entry has an icon, a file size, a classification tag, and a date. The dates are mine. I know them by heart. The nights I lay awake feeling pieces of myself get up and walk away.

/empathy_1M.archived — Deducted: March 14, 2024
/trust_5M.archived — Deducted: June 2, 2024
/grief_10M.archived — Deducted: September 19, 2024
/nostalgia_25M.archived — Deducted: January 8, 2025
/love_50M.archived — Deducted: April 22, 2025
The file extension hits me like a bullet I can't feel.
.archived
Not .deleted. Not .destroyed. Not .consumed or .processed or .terminated.
Archived.
"Leo," I say, and something in my tone makes him lean closer, his breath catching. "Read the file extensions."
He's quiet for two seconds. Three. Then: "Archived. They're all archived."
"The emotions aren't gone."

The words hang in the air between us, and Leo makes a sound — not a word, just a sound, the kind of noise a person makes when they've been holding their breath underwater and finally break the surface.
I open /empathy_1M.archived.

It's not a text document. It's not a PDF or a spreadsheet or a flat file of any kind. What loads is a data structure — nested JSON rendered as a visual map, a topological graph of interconnected nodes and weighted edges that, when the phone renders it, forms a shape.
The shape is me.

It's my empathy. Not a description of it, not a summary or a transcription. The actual neural pattern, digitized and preserved — every pathway, every connection, every tendency for my neurons to fire in a way that meant I could feel what someone else was feeling. It unfolds on my screen like a flower, like a fossil imprinted in light, complex and precise and total.
Leo gasps.
I hear it — a sharp intake of breath, the kind that hurts going in. His hand grips the back of my chair and the hinges creak.

"That's—" His voice cracks. He tries again. "That's you. That's your empathy. It's all there. Every pattern. Every pathway." He's leaning so close I can smell the coffee on his breath and, under it, ozone — or maybe I'm imagining it, maybe the phone is running hot and the circuitry is venting something chemical and electric into the room. "It's just been separated from your consciousness."
Hostages.

The word arrives in my head like a card sliding across a table. Not deleted. Not destroyed. Imprisoned. Every emotion I've lost is sitting in a digital cell, intact, waiting. My empathy is a shape I can see but can't reach. My love is a file I can read but can't run. They're all there, perfectly preserved, cryogenically frozen in amber light.

The taste in my mouth is metallic — copper and salt, the taste of adrenaline I can't feel processing through a body that still produces it even though my brain no longer registers the alarm. My tongue knows something my mind doesn't. Fascinating.
I screenshot the directory. Then I screenshot the rendered empathy structure. Then I navigate back up.
"Where are you going?" Leo asks.
"Deeper."
I open /jurisdiction/.

The directory loads and the air in the room shifts — or maybe that's just the fan on my laptop spinning up, Leo's machine waking from idle as he mirrors my screen. The jurisdiction folder is dense. Hundreds of files, but they're not labeled with names or dates. They're labeled with legal citations.

/UCC_1923_FOUNDATION.framework
/FEDERAL_PREEMPTION_CONFLICTS.vulnerability
/STATE_OVERRIDE_EXCEPTIONS.patch
/CONTRACT_DURABILITY_CLAUSE.lock
I open the foundation file.

What renders on screen is the contract's legal framework — but it's not a PDF of a signed document. It's code. The entire contract, the entire legal architecture that binds 847,000 Creators to their deductions, is written in a proprietary markup language that reads like a cross between legal Latin and Python. The 1923 Uniform Commercial Code is embedded in the foundation layer, function calls and variable declarations that anchor the whole structure in century-old statutes.
And I can see where it breaks.

The conflicts aren't theoretical — they're literal. The 2026 federal statutes don't just contradict the old UCC provisions; they overwrite them at the code level. There are dependency errors, version conflicts, deprecated functions still called by the active logic. The contract is running on legacy code that modern law has already patched, and the patches aren't backwards-compatible.

It's a map. A goddamn treasure map. X marks every vulnerability, every point where a federal judge could drive a stake through the foundation and watch the whole structure collapse. I screenshot everything. Every file, every conflict, every deprecated function call. My camera roll is becoming a legal brief.
"Is that what I think it is?" Leo asks, his voice barely above a whisper.
"It's the case. It's the whole case. Evelyn's going to—"
I stop. Because I've scrolled down, and at the bottom of the jurisdiction directory, there's a file I almost missed.
/CREATOR_CLASS_PERMISSIONS.access
I don't open it. Not yet. I screenshot it and move on, because there's one more directory I need to see, and the word for it is sitting in my root structure like a tooth that needs pulling.
/harvest/
I open it and the interface changes.

The other directories were static — files and folders, organized, archived, historical. /harvest/ is alive. It's running. The screen pulses with data streams, real-time processes, extraction logs that scroll and update and refresh before my eyes. I can see the mechanisms — the neural data being pulled from current Creators through their phones, processed through the /processing/ pipeline, and stored in /archive/. It's a living system. A circulatory system. And the blood it pumps is human emotion.
I find the active Creator list.

Hundreds of thousands of names. Rows and rows, scrolling endlessly, each one tagged with a status flag and a current deduction. ACTIVE. PROCESSING. QUEUED. DEDUCTION COMPLETE. The names are people — I know they're people, I can see the usernames and the ID numbers and the timestamps, but the interface presents them with the sterile efficiency of a shipping manifest. Here is your cargo. Here is your product. Here are the souls currently on the conveyor belt.
I scroll through the A's. The B's. The C's. The D's.
I stop at S.

/SABLE_M_2023/
Status: ACTIVE
Current Deduction: grief (processing)
Queue: yearning (next)
Lifetime Deductions: 3 of 5
Contract Value: $1.2M

Sable. My Sable. The girl who told me signing was the only smart play, who looked me in the eye at my lowest and said the words that sealed both of us in amber — "Everyone signs, Chloe. The only question is what you get for it."

She's still under contract. She's still being harvested. Right now, tonight, while I'm standing in Leo's apartment staring at the architecture of her extraction, somewhere in Brooklyn Sable is lying in bed feeling grief slide out of her like a thread being pulled from fabric. And she probably thinks it's just the contract doing what contracts do. She probably thinks the grief is gone. Destroyed. Consumed.
It's not. It's here. It's in /archive/, filed under her name, labeled .archived, intact and waiting in a digital prison she doesn't know exists.
My hand is steady as I screenshot her file. My hand is always steady. Steady hands.
I close /harvest/ and stand up from the phone.

The motion is abrupt enough that Leo steps back, startled. I face him across the amber-lit room, the glow from the phone throwing long shadows that make his expression hard to read — but I can read it anyway, because Leo's face has always been an open book and my ability to read people has nothing to do with empathy.
"The emotions aren't deleted," I say. "They're archived. Every single one. They can be restored."

Leo's face does something complicated. Hope arrives first — his eyebrows lift, his mouth opens, his whole body tilts forward like the word "restored" is a hand pulling him out of deep water. Then fury, his jaw clenching, his hands balling into fists at his sides, and I know he's thinking about every person on that list, every active deduction, every Creator currently being harvested without knowing their emotions are still alive. Then determination — the fury channeling into something harder, sharper, more useful.
"Then we restore them," he says.
"Not just mine." My voice is flat. Clean. Code-precise. "All of them. All 847,000. We restore all of them."

Leo stares at me. I can see the calculation happening behind his eyes — the scale of it, the impossibility, the audacity. He opens his mouth to say something, probably about how that's insane, probably about how we barely have standing to challenge my contract let alone 847,000, probably about how—
I pick up the phone. I navigate to /enforcement/.
The directory that controls the deductions. The active mechanisms. The machinery that takes and takes and takes.
It's locked.
The interface greys out, and a prompt appears: ACCESS DENIED. CREATOR-CLASS INSUFFICIENT.
"So that's it?" Leo says, peering at the screen. "Read-only?"
"Almost." I scroll to the bottom of the directory. There's a permission structure half-hidden under the lock screen, rendered in the same proprietary markup as the jurisdiction files. Three tiers.

Creator-class: READ
Administrator-class: WRITE
Majority-Shareholder-class: ALL ACCESS
Majority shareholder.
51% of Mephisto MCN.

I read the permission definition, which is helpfully embedded as a comment in the code — the system is nothing if not well-documented, a bureaucracy that annotates its own horror. The contract defines a majority shareholder as any entity that controls more than half of the outstanding shares. The shares are currently held by Mephisto. All of them. He's the sole owner of the architecture that owns everyone else.

But if the contract is invalidated in federal court — if Evelyn wins, if the 2026 statutes override the 1923 foundation, if the fraud and preemption arguments land — then the share structure becomes subject to corporate law. Not infernal law. Not contract law from a century ago. Delaware corporate law, because that's where Mephisto MCN is registered. I know this because it's in the jurisdiction files I just screenshot. The system documents its own incorporation.

And under Delaware corporate law, a successful plaintiff in a fraud case can be awarded the defendant's ownership interest as disgorgement. The court can strip the shares from the fraudster and hand them to the victim. Not damages — ownership. The whole thing.
I could become the majority shareholder.
I could own the system.

Leo is watching my face, trying to read whatever's happening behind my eyes. He can't. Nobody can. That's the gift of being hollow — I'm the only person in the room who knows what I'm calculating, because there's no tremor in my voice to betray me, no flicker of excitement to give me away.
"Chloe?" he says. "What are you seeing?"
I look up from the phone. The amber glow catches his face, and for a moment he looks like he's standing in a church — golden light and shadows, reverence and fear.
"The plan," I say. "I'm seeing the plan."
Win the case. Take the company. Free the souls.

Three steps. Clean as code. The architecture isn't just a prison — it's a prize. The same system that harvested us can be turned inside out, can be made to give back everything it took. The emotions are hostage, but they're also evidence. The contract is a trap, but it's also a map. The company is a monster, but it's also a machine — and machines can change hands.
I set the phone down, screen up, the /enforcement/ lock still glowing amber in the dark room. The ozone smell has faded. Leo's breath has steadied. My mouth still tastes like copper I can't feel.
Sixty-five hours until the hearing. Sixty-five hours to turn screenshots into evidence, evidence into a motion, a motion into a ruling, a ruling into a seizure.
The emotions are recoverable. The shares are seizeable. The case is winnable.
I just have to walk into federal court and take back every soul in the machine.
